examples.title: Secure SQL Query with Parameterized Statements
examples.description: Demonstrates secure database queries using parameterized statements to prevent SQL injection
examples.code:
import sqlite3

# VULNERABLE - Never do this!
def insecure_query(user_input):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('app.db')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '{user_input}'"
    cursor.execute(query)  # SQL INJECTION POSSIBLE!
    return cursor.fetchall()

# SECURE - Using parameterized query
def secure_query(user_input):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('app.db')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?"
    cursor.execute(query, (user_input,))  # Parameters bound safely
    return cursor.fetchall()

# SECURE - Using parameterized query with named parameters
def secure_query_named(user_input):
    conn = sqlite3.connect('app.db')
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = :name"
    cursor.execute(query, {"name": user_input})
    return cursor.fetchall()
examples.explanation: The parameterized query approach treats user input as literal data, preventing attackers from manipulating the SQL structure. The '?' placeholder is bound to the parameter value, making injection impossible.

examples.title: Secure Password Hashing
examples.description: Demonstrates secure password storage using bcrypt hashing
examples.explanation: BCrypt automatically generates a random salt for each password and uses adaptive cost factors to make hashing computationally expensive, protecting against brute force and rainbow table attacks.

examples.title: Safe String Handling to Prevent Buffer Overflow
examples.description: Demonstrates secure string operations in C to prevent buffer overflow vulnerabilities
examples.explanation: Buffer overflow occurs when data exceeds allocated memory boundaries. Safe practices include: using bounded string functions, always ensuring null termination, allocating exact needed size, and checking allocations.

examples.title: XSS Prevention with Output Encoding
examples.description: Demonstrates proper output encoding to prevent Cross-Site Scripting attacks
examples.explanation: Output encoding converts dangerous characters into safe HTML entities, preventing browsers from interpreting user input as executable code. Using textContent instead of innerHTML provides automatic encoding.
